analysis.note: Locative adverb, “there/in that”; final -a contracts with the following initial a-.
Neuter nominative singular agreeing with tat; initial a- is absorbed in tatrā-, and final -m appears as -ñ before ca in the bhāṣya reading.

verification.detail: compound stem anivṛtāvyākṛta as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Neuter nominative singular pronoun; subject of the implied copula with anivṛtāvyākṛtam.
Adverb, “likewise/so too,” carrying the preceding predication forward elliptically.
“Contact and the rest”; the bhāṣya reading leaves -aḥ before tac ca, unlike the fully sandhied vulgate -as tac ca.

verification.detail: compound stem sparśādi as Pum/Prathama/Bahu: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Neuter nominative singular; not construed with the masculine plural sparśādayaḥ, but as the singular subject of vartate.
Instrumental singular; external sandhi srotasā + oghavat gives printed srotasaughavat, with ā + o → au.
Neuter nominative singular adjective/predicate, “flood-bearing/torrential”; its initial o is represented in the preceding au of srotasaughavat.

justifications.decision: Construe the two feminine nominative singulars predicatively rather than as a single compound or as two coordinated subjects.
justifications.chosen: upekṣā is a predicate nominative and vedanā the subject: “the feeling is equanimity.”

evidence.translation: Both upekṣā and vedanā are feminine nominative singular; with no overt verb, the natural construal is an implied copula: “there, feeling is equanimity.”

justifications.decision: Read anivṛta + avyākṛta as a compound meaning “unobstructed/uncovered and indeterminate/neutral,” not as anivṛtā feminine plus avyākṛtam neuter.
options_considered: split anivṛtā as feminine nominative singular and avyākṛtam as neuter
read anivṛtāvyākṛtam as one neuter compound predicated of tat
read the compound as a dvandva
justifications.chosen: anivṛtāvyākṛtam is one neuter singular karmadhāraya compound agreeing with tat.

evidence.translation: The final -ñ before ca points to an underlying neuter -am; the following tat is neuter nominative singular, supporting agreement with anivṛtāvyākṛtam.

justifications.decision: The plural sparśādayaḥ is elliptical with tathā; it is not made into a kind of vedanā, since sparśa ‘contact’ is distinct from feeling.
options_considered: “contact and the rest are likewise feelings of equanimity”
“contact and the rest are likewise unobstructed-indeterminate”
justifications.chosen: tathā makes sparśādayaḥ inherit the immediately preceding qualification: “contact and the rest are likewise [unobstructed-indeterminate].”

evidence.translation: The adverb tathā follows the predication anivṛtāvyākṛtam ca tat and precedes sparśādayaḥ, so it most naturally carries that predication forward: “so too contact and the rest.”

justifications.decision: Because sparśādayaḥ is masculine plural, neuter singular tac cannot grammatically refer to it as its subject; vartate is singular.
justifications.chosen: The second tac is neuter singular subject of vartate, referring to the same singular ‘that,’ not to sparśādayaḥ.

evidence.translation: The pronoun tac and the verb vartate are singular; sparśādayaḥ is plural. Hence tac ca vartate must resume a neuter singular antecedent.

justifications.decision: Translate the phrase idiomatically as a current/torrent simile while retaining the grammatical split: instrumental srotasā plus neuter oghavat.
options_considered: sandhi resolution srotasā oghavat, “flood-like with a current”
treat oghavat as a separate possessive adjective agreeing with tac
justifications.chosen: srotasaughavat is resolved as srotasā oghavat in this ablation, following the analyzer and regular ā + o → au sandhi.

evidence.translation: The printed au is directly explained by srotasā + oghavat. Thus the words can be read as “it continues, flood-like/torrential, with a current.”

one_shot_delta: Without commentary, the antecedent of tat remains inferred only from grammar and the preceding context not supplied here; a blind translation could wrongly attach tac ca vartate to the plural sparśādayaḥ.
A dictionary-only reading could take upekṣā as mere ‘neglect/indifference’ rather than the technical affective tone ‘equanimity.’
The scope of tathā is uncertain without commentary; grammar favors carrying forward anivṛtāvyākṛtam to sparśādayaḥ, but a blind reading might instead connect it with upekṣā vedanā.

The final simile is especially vulnerable: the analyzer-supported split srotasā oghavat is phonologically regular, while many interpretive translations may prefer the compound-like idiom “like a stream-current.”
analyzer_disagreements: The api_seg output inserts ca after vedanā and omits tatra; I follow the supplied verse and the local_S segmentation, reading vedanā tatra, not vedanā ca.

ByT5 separates anivṛtā and avyākṛtam; I analyze anivṛtāvyākṛtam as a single neuter nominative singular karmadhāraya compound agreeing with tat. The split feminine anivṛtā would not construe with neuter tat/avyākṛtam.
ByT5 represents sparśādayaḥ as sparśa_Cp + ādayaḥ; I record the same analysis as the inflected compound stem sparśādi, since the verse has the single compound form sparśādayaḥ.
open_questions: No commentary was supplied in this ablation, so the exact scholastic force of anivṛta and avyākṛta cannot be confirmed from trbh evidence.
The singular antecedent of tat is grammatically clear as neuter singular, but its doctrinal referent depends on the preceding verse/context not included in the ablation data.
Whether srotasaughavat should ultimately be read as the sandhi of srotasā oghavat or as an idiomatized compound-like simile needs commentary or manuscript evidence beyond the ablation instructions.
